description: Hand-authored synthetic documentation corpus for the Aurora API platform. Fixed, stable chunk ids. Used as the gold-evidence retrieval benchmark corpus for Module 21b Task 1.

chunks.text: Create an API key from the Aurora dashboard under Settings, Keys. Send it on every request in the Authorization Bearer header. Each key belongs to one project.
Rotate an API key by generating a new one and revoking the old key. During rotation the previous key stays valid for a 24 hour grace period so deployments can roll over without downtime.
For server-to-server access Aurora supports the OAuth2 client credentials grant. Exchange your client id and client secret at the token endpoint to receive a short-lived access token.
OAuth access tokens expire 60 minutes after they are issued. Request a new token with your client credentials before the old one expires; there is no refresh token in the client credentials flow.
Every API key is granted one or more scopes such as read, write, or admin. A request that needs a scope the key lacks is rejected with a 403 error.
Request quotas depend on your plan: Free allows 60 requests per minute, Pro allows 600, and Enterprise is negotiated. Quotas are counted per project.
The rate limiter uses a token bucket that refills continuously, so short bursts above your steady rate are allowed as long as the bucket still has tokens.
When you exceed your quota Aurora returns HTTP 429. The response includes a Retry-After header telling you how many seconds to wait before retrying.
Every response carries X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, and X-RateLimit-Reset headers so clients can throttle themselves before hitting a 429.
Aurora has three plans. Free costs nothing, Pro is 49 dollars per month, and Enterprise is custom priced. Upgrading takes effect immediately and is prorated.
Usage is metered per successful API call. Calls that return a 4xx client error are not billed, but 2xx and 3xx responses count toward your monthly usage.
If you pass your plan included call volume, extra calls are billed as overage at 0.5 cents each. You can set a spending cap to block requests once a limit is reached.
Invoices are generated on the first day of each month and cover the previous month. Download past invoices as PDF from the Billing section of the dashboard.
Choose a data residency region when you create a project: us-east, eu-west, or ap-south. Data stored in a project never leaves its region.
All objects are encrypted at rest with AES-256 and in transit with TLS 1.2 or higher. Encryption keys are managed by Aurora and rotated automatically.
Aurora takes an automated backup of your data every 24 hours. Backups are kept for 30 days, after which they are permanently deleted.
Deleted objects are held in a recoverable trash for 7 days before permanent removal. You can restore an object from trash through the API or the dashboard.
Each webhook delivery is signed with an HMAC-SHA256 signature in the Aurora-Signature header. Verify it against your endpoint signing secret to confirm the payload is authentic.
If your endpoint does not return a 2xx status, Aurora retries the webhook with exponential backoff for up to 24 hours before giving up and marking the delivery failed.
Webhooks can notify you of events such as object.created, object.deleted, and invoice.paid. Subscribe to specific event types when you register an endpoint.
The Enterprise plan carries a 99.9 percent monthly uptime SLA. If uptime falls below that, you receive service credits proportional to the downtime.
Support response targets depend on plan: Free uses community forums, Pro gets email support within one business day, and Enterprise gets a 1 hour response for critical issues.
During an incident Aurora posts updates to the public status page and emails project owners. A post-incident review is published within five business days.
Official Aurora SDKs are available for Python, JavaScript/TypeScript, Go, and Ruby. All SDKs wrap the same REST API and read the API key from an environment variable.
SDKs follow semantic versioning. A deprecated method keeps working for at least two minor releases and logs a warning before it is removed in a major release.
List endpoints return at most 100 items per page and use cursor-based pagination. Follow the next_cursor field to fetch the following page until it is null.
Errors return a JSON body with a code, a human readable message, and a request_id. Include the request id when you contact support about a failed call.
Make a POST safe to retry by sending an Idempotency-Key header. Aurora stores the first response for that key for 24 hours and replays it instead of running the action twice.
